text:
Depending on the age, you could swap out the old hard drive and put in an SSD or even add an nvme drive. 
This would result in much faster start up times. Sometimes increasing the RAM may also give you a decent boost without too much investment.
Thank you for making this video - due to our education system, many people are afraid to modify or 'fix' technological devices. This video now, a month or years later will enable someone to get the confidence to actually 'give it a go'.

author: @SimonPlatten
text: Linux and other OS's other than Windoz show how retarded the hardware is by running Windows.  The thing to be aware of before proceeding is make sure you can get all the hardware drivers for your laptop before you go ahead, every operating system is going to need the correct drivers for the target hardware.  In some cases this may mean that you will loose the display if video drivers are not available or you can't use the correct resolution because only the basic VGA driver is available which means only 640x480 maximum resolution.

author: @ronnyb5890
text: sadely,linux cant run windows specific programs,of wich i have a lot of,you have to use WINE,which will slow the performance of the computer,so you're back at square one,and what about security,windows has built in security with defender,what does linux have? i hate to pay a yearly subscription for anti-virus software,however,as you said,this is great for students as they only need openoffice or something like that to do some school work

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Linux security model with user rights group and admin rights is already more secure than windows. And then there are many applications and linux services that can further enhance security.  

Linux has some of the fastest firewalls.  Maybe only FreeBSD beats linux in firewalls. 

I use linux on a USB flash drive with linux anti virus software to find  window's viruses on infected windows computers without booting into windows. On linux, anti virus software is typically used to identify and clean up viruses stored on windows file shares and sent through email servers.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
For simple clarification, Linux while very similar to Unix, was never and can never be called Unix. Instead we can call Linux "Unix like", just not call it Unix. There are a great many articles to be found explaining this point and why the distinction. 😁

I used to run a SVR4 Unix os on my old sparcs before switching to Linux, and then OpenBSD for better stability on the sparc.

Its just the common hardware support, if you want to run old or limited hardware, most likley you need to recompile your own drivers to your core.... Running old hardware is also a security issue.

author: @totherarf
text:
There is another aspect of Linux that you did not touch on ...... Virus's!
Being open source there is little you need to do in the anti-virus department ..... and updates are a breeze (click on the shield icon on the bottom bar) and it will do everything in the background!

Also .... you can play! Seriously there are many distro's (versions) of Linux that offer eather bleeding edge or super stable long term service. Dont like the one you have ..... chose another. Eather a full install or flash it to a USB and run from there leaving your existing system intact wile you play around!  

And the killer reason to change ...... It is FREE!
Not just Linux itself but many of the programmes too!

author: @tims8603
text: I've been sing Linux since 2007. I'm not a Linux guru but I got used to it fairly easily. You should tell people that, since it's a completely different OS, you need to use the repository to install apps/programs. I think that some people have trouble understanding that.  There are ways to run Windows programs but it's more complicated. Most people will find everything they need in the repositories.

replies.text: Have you tried synaptic package manager

replies.author: @tims8603

replies.author: @robertpitt8418
replies.text: @tims8603 i thought you might have been using the terminal and the software centre only, and have not yet tried synaptic .While i am still  here have you ever tried pcmanfm  file manager it has dual pane mode with full drag and drop function, i find it much easier to use than the built in file manager. Cheers

replies.author: @tims8603
replies.text: @robertpitt8418 My regular Linux is PCLinuxOS, it doesn't have Software Manager. I have other machines that use Ubuntu derivatives so I am aware of PCMan and have used it. PCLinuxOS uses Dolphin and also can use split windows with full drag and drop.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
It is also possible to compile from source code without using a package manager... But it can get awkward to remove the software later. And tbh while I been messing with Linux on and off since about 2000,  only time I've ever had to do that was within last month to try a utility obtained from git-hub  to change a setting for an embeded  hardware raid on a machine. (like a bios setting... But in the embedded controller itself) 

Before their instructions reputedly got hopelessly out of date... I used to use gentoo... That uses a package manager to compile source code... Learnt so much using it.. And even now wonder why some insist it's difficult (it ain't if one followes the instructions rather than knowing better. OK it's command line install by typing stuff, but in typing it one remembers stuff/learns what things do etc) That ran even faster as well as code can be optimised for specific machine/other programs already there , *and* I found discovered the source code for say open office often has optional extra features (like solvers for quite  complex math  problems) 
Which just don't get included in the standard packaged up compilation... Just too many possible combos/if everything was included would take up loads extra space.

replies.author: @tristan4777
replies.text: Not all software available for Windows is available for Linux. Depending on what you want to run you could try WINE. There may also be free alternatives.

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text:
Couple of questions please mate. I don’t know how Linux works – is it best to create a small partition for the OS to sit in, and have the rest of the hard drive for docs for security? Or just let it do its thing when installing?

Is Linux mint better than Ubuntu?

Was going to ask, but it says you can install mobile apps to the laptop! If that so ... that’s awesome, nice one!

All the best bro, kieron

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Ubuntu vs Mint is largely a preference thing.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
One can write a book on Partitioning optimization. But, your light years ahead already if you just go with the defaults when you put linux on.

Partitioning Optimization  is dependent on many things, too many to list in a short comment. Partitioning optimizations are often less important on a desktop, but can certainly help in some circumstances. Servers with many users is where optimizations become more important and have greater impact. 

Just touching on a few things considered when deciding how to optimize partitions. Ssd vs hard drives with platters. To put a partition on the outside vs inside. should one raid, and if so what kind of raid,  do one want the fastest boot or fastest access to user files,  how much memory and does one use swap alot or not. 

If your just getting started you might try KISS rule, and go with the defaults. You can optimize later as you learn, and  for how you us your system.

replies.author: @tristan4777
replies.text: It is a personal preference thing mainly. Ubuntu is quite useable for the home user but has business support ( via Canonical the company  behind it). Mint is derived from Ubuntu but intended to be a friendly system for a home user rather than a business tool. I used Ubuntu for years and it was great but I currently use Mint, among others.

replies.author: @tristan4777
replies.text: Regarding partitioning unless you have a specific need the default will work fine. Never used anything else.

replies.author: @bulwinkle
replies.text: Mint is a fork of Ubuntu.

author: @kristiangibson106
text: Another good thing to do that won't put you back too much is to buy a sata SSD. They are compatible with any system with a sata port and have a much larger cache and faster speed than those slow 5400 rpm drives that are installed in most older laptops. You will be going from a 100mbps drive to a 550mbps drive. This means that boot times will improve significantly as well as the times to open software or large files. You could also increase the amount of memory in the system but thats only needed if you find you are running out of memory during multitasking or while running more demanding software that eats it all up. Linux is definitely a great option for older laptops though, especially with the amount of systems that are going to be incompatible with windows 11. Linux is also great for security reasons since you could always run a virtual machine for an extra layer of protection.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Linux isn't for everyone, there is a big learning curve for some people, especially the older generation sbusfetgvm to windows OS

replies.author: @bulwinkle
replies.text: I'm over 70 and am a regular linux user, so leave us wrinklies alone.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Certainly not for everyone with a particular need of certain software, and given most people really don't like change.  I will disagree on the huge learning curve, because there are many distributions that broke down that barrier.  And given the cost, increased productivity because of  increased performance and reliability and security, gives many people reason to just go for it and take the plunge. 

Or, one can just throw away their old uselessly slow equipment, all because one is just too stubborn to try something new.  The point of the video was how to breath new life into an old computer equipment. Implicit in that is that it's no longer useful. And this video is on how to make it useful again at no cost.

replies.author: @bulwinkle
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 there is very little that you can't do in Linux unless you are tied to some proprietary software that will only runs in Windoze, or you are a gamer, but that is changing quite quickly.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @bulwinkle agreed, I was referring to being tied to proprietary software.  But yes, as a general rule of thumb, Any thing you can do on MS Winblows you can do on linux, exception being sometimes you can't run certain proprietary  software.  I've been a linux user for maybe 25 years or more. Started with trying out redhat and debian on a sparc classic before moving onto openbsd for that machine.  Ran linux on old 386,486 and almost  everything there after.

replies.author: @bulwinkle
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 yes, I started with Red Hat in 1994. Funnily enough I sometimes sport a red fedora.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Including all devices, Linux is probably the most used operating system in the world.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: It is an awesome os! Im even thinking about putting Ubuntu on my main computer. maybe dual boot with windows?

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Linux absolutely breathes new life into old hardware. I've been using Linux exclusively for maybe 25 years now. Finally after maybe 20 years of trying, i convinced my now 75 year old father to install Linux on his 10 minute to boot windows laptop, that now runs Ubuntu Mate with an improved  boot time at around 50 seconds, and then with a new SSD further improved the boot time to less than 30 seconds.  Basic desktop use like browsing the net,  checking email, spreadsheet  and word processing are incredibly  intuitive making the switch for him incredibly easy, and he was very much happer with his improved productivity. Yes you can teach an old dog new tricks.  It certainly helped that i got him to use firefox and libreoffice years ago so his transition to Linux went very smooth.

replies.author: @grahamepigney8565
replies.text:
Before changing to Linux replacing HDD(s) with SSD(s) and making sure that the PC has the maximum anount of memory installed is essential.

Like for like Linux is less demanding on the hardware than Windows 10 but even these days Linux is more suitable for techies. Changing BIOS options and the like isn't simple and many people will be find installing "stuff" and deleting "stuff" a daunting prospect.

I'm a techie with 40 plus years experience of installing and updating an OS (operating system) but I wouldn't recommend switching to Linux unless the user was rather adventurous or had somebody at his/her side who could walk them through the difficulties/technicalites involved.

Other issues about how you move files/infrmation to Linux were ignored, continuity is one of the big issues as far as most people are concerned - difficult enough to do from one Windows PC to another Windows PC even more difficult when switching from Windows to Linux.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @grahamepigney8565 replace hard drive with SSD, and installing more ram is surely helpful, but not essential, as one can still do better than their old system with a free and fresh install of linux, and preferably a low resource desktop environment.

author: @A65Bill
text: How about a 32 bit machine - anything available, please?

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Yes, there are many 32 bit versions  of various linux distributions that are available for download.  All you need to do is search Google. Download a few and give them a try to find out your favorite. 

BUT, some software is no longer supported on 32 bit hardware like Google Chrome, which means that particular software is no longer updated with bug fixes security updates and enhanced features. But many times you can find alternatives that will do the same.

replies.author: @tristan4777
replies.text: There is a 32bit version of Mint (the distro Luke was using) and as Keith says there are lots of other distros that have a 32bit option. I've got SparkyLinux running a Pentium 4 PC I use as a server. I've also got a very old laptop currently running Zorin (not the Ultimate Edition). People can get quite excited about their favourite Distro but Mint is a great place to start if you're new to it all.

replies.author: @tims8603
replies.text: Also, give Bodhi 32 bit a try. It's very light on resources. Remember, you can run Linux from a USB stick or DVD to see if it works before installing.

replies.author: @veronicathecow
replies.text: There are lots of versions of Puppy linux, my favourite for low power or old PCs
